coredata.dc:title: O, S-g-C3N4 nanotubes as photovoltaic boosters in quantum dot-sensitized all-weather solar cells: a synergistic approach for enhanced power conversion efficiency in dark-light conditions
coredata.dc:creator: Riaz, S.
Park, S.-J.
coredata.dc:description: All up-to-the-minute solar cells generate highly efficient electricity under sunlight. However, the power outputs are ∼ zero under dark conditions due to the relatively low visible light intensity. We report high-performance quantum dots-sanitized all-weather solar cells (QDSSCs). The devices were based on rationally designed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated oxygen and sulfur co-doped graphitic carbon nitride nanotubes (O, S-g-C3N4 NTs)-sensitized TiO2/long-persistence phosphor (LPP) photoanodes and Pt counter electrodes (CE). O, S-doped-g-C3N4 NTs can act as photo-boosters as well as the blocking layer to prevent the backflow of photoexcited electrons and prevent their recombination with the electrolyte. LPPs can store unabsorbed light ranging from visible to infrared light across the photoanode and convert it into green fluorescence, ultimately persistent CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs irradiation, and electricity generation under sunlight illumination in all light-dark environments. The maximum PCE was 18.5% for the device 7-CC/L prepared with a composite of CQDs and O, S-g-C3N4 NTs with an optimized weight of 7 mg and 0.2 g, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar energy
Stability
Fluorescence
Photoanode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of O, S-doped g-C3N4 NTs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (No. 2022M3J7A1062940). This research was also supported by Korea Electric Power Corporation (Grant number : R21XO01-5).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesis of O, S-g-C3N4 NTs from single precursor.
(a) UV–Vis spectra (b) images of corresponding samples, (c) Kubelka-Munk plot, and (d) PL spectra of O, S-g-C3N4 NTs and CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 samples (at λex = 325 nm).
XPS survey scan, (a) high-resolution O1s (b), S2p(c), C1s (d), and N1s, (e) spectra of O, S-g-C3N4 NTs.
TEM images of (a) O, S-g-C3N4 NTs (inset: HR-TEM image), (b) CQDs (inset: HR-TEM and size distribution), and (c) 7-C/CN.
XRD patterns of CQDs, O, S-g-C3N4 NTs, and CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs samples.

(a) All-weather solar cell assembly. (b) Photoluminescence images of (1st row) FTO/TiO2/5-C/CN/LPP, (2nd row) FTO/TiO2/7-C/CN/LPP, and (3rd row) FTO/TiO2/9-C/CN/LPP photoanodes after irradiation by simulated sunlight for 60 s.
(a) Optical transmittance spectra of all the prepared photoanodes. (b) PL emission spectra of FTO/TiO2/5-C/CN/LPP, FTO/TiO2/7-C/CN/LPP, and FTO/TiO2/9-C/CN/LPP photoanodes.
Representative (a and b) photo and (c) dark J-V characteristics of the solar cells.
Representative photo J-V characteristics of the solar cells as different decay times.

(a) Afterglow intensity of an m-TiO2/LPP photoanode monitored by using a standard silicon solar cell as a function of the decay time. (b) Dark Jsc decay versus time. (c) Dark Voc decay versus time. (d) Dark FF decay versus time.
Photovoltaic parameters of all-weather solar cells under 100 mW/cm2 intensity light irradiation.
Photovoltaic parameters of all-weather solar cells under dark conditions.
A comparison of obtained efficiency with the already published literature.

head.ce:title: O, S-g-C3N4 nanotubes as photovoltaic boosters in quantum dot-sensitized all-weather solar cells: a synergistic approach for enhanced power conversion efficiency in dark-light conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Inha University, 100 Inharo, Incheon, 22212, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Inha University, 100 Inharo, Incheon, 22212, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: All up-to-the-minute solar cells generate highly efficient electricity under sunlight. However, the power outputs are ∼ zero under dark conditions due to the relatively low visible light intensity. We report high-performance quantum dots-sanitized all-weather solar cells (QDSSCs). The devices were based on rationally designed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated oxygen and sulfur co-doped graphitic carbon nitride nanotubes (O, S-g-C3N4 NTs)-sensitized TiO2/long-persistence phosphor (LPP) photoanodes and Pt counter electrodes (CE). O, S-doped-g-C3N4 NTs can act as photo-boosters as well as the blocking layer to prevent the backflow of photoexcited electrons and prevent their recombination with the electrolyte. LPPs can store unabsorbed light ranging from visible to infrared light across the photoanode and convert it into green fluorescence, ultimately persistent CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs irradiation, and electricity generation under sunlight illumination in all light-dark environments. The maximum PCE was 18.5% for the device 7-CC/L prepared with a composite of CQDs and O, S-g-C3N4 NTs with an optimized weight of 7 mg and 0.2 g, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy crises have become a global problem due to the increasing energy demand and environmental concerns [1]. Solar energy, as clean, green, and environment-friendly energy, is superior in coping with the aforementioned issues [2]. Different types of thin-film photovoltaic (PV) devices—lightweight, flexible, fibrous, and tubular-shaped—have been developed for silicon solar cells [3–6]. These solar cells produce highly efficient electricity by absorbing photons under sunlight irradiation. However, it is significantly challenging for up-to-the-minute solar cells to produce electricity on sunny days and in low-light or dark environments. Researchers focused on developing sun- or rain-triggered solar cells in the past by combining platinum alloys, graphene, or graphene tailored conductive coatings with the conventional solar cell devices, yielding a maximum power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 10.38% under 100 mW/cm2 illumination [7–9]. However, these solar cells had two major drawbacks: (1) The graphene-based materials or the Pt alloys were always installed on the backside of the solar cells without sacrificing PCE during sunny days. It means that the rain-sensitive film had to be turned upward during the rainy days, adding to a practically fussy process; (2) These sun and rain-triggered solar cells could not produce electricity at night or in other low-light environments [10]. Therefore, there has been a great demand for all-weather solar cells.

The study of non-toxic, low-cost, and environment-friendly semiconductor materials with high all-weather solar energy conversion has become fascinating to reduce the use of fossil fuels and fulfill the clean energy demand [11]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), an emerging member of the carbon family, have attracted the attention of researchers [12]. CQDs have shown numerous applications in fields, such as PVs, bioimaging, and light-emitting diodes, due to their tunable electronic properties, excellent photostability, low cost, and less toxicity [13–15]. CQDs combined with polymers are generally used in solar cells to sensitize TiO2 or as an electron acceptor, active layer dopant, and hole transport layer in organic solar cells [16–20]. Recently, researchers have reported on the use of CQDs in solar cells as a lone light absorber—without a co-sensitizer—with a maximum PCE of 1.20% [21].

Nanostructured graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4)—a carbonaceous, light, and non-metal semiconductor—has been attracted significant attention in recent years owing to its narrow bandgap (∼2.7), chemical stability, and low cost [22–24]. Furthermore, it is widely used as a photocatalyst [25]. Recent studies have shown the excellent photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 in visible light [26,27]. However, only a few studies have applied g-C3N4 in PV devices. Experimental and theoretical studies have shown that pristine bulk g-C3N4 has limited visible light absorption regions (<460 nm), poor quantum efficiency, and radiative recombination rate of photogenerated electrons (e) and holes (h+) [28,29]. Bulk g-C3N4 is not beneficial in solar energy conversion owing to the high recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and less active sites [30]. Many methods, such as doping with metal or non-metal ions, developing heterojunctions with other semiconductors, and co-sensitization with dyes, have been adopted to overcome the abovementioned problems [31–33]. The doping method, especially non-metal doping, is essential due to its effectiveness in broadening the optical response range of semiconductor materials [34]. Studies also proved that CQDs coupling with g-C3N4 can enhance visible light absorption beyond 600 nm and provide high quantum efficiency [35,36]. Theoretical studies showed that type-II van der Waals heterojunction could be developed between CQDs and g-C3N4, and CQDs can act as spectral sensitizers [29,37]. However, researchers reported excellent photocatalytic performance by CQDs/g-C3N4 composites with different morphologies of g-C3N4, demonstrating a facile electronic interaction in the composite [38–40]. There is a need to explore the influence of g-C3N4 on the performance of PV devices. Hence, numerous morphological nanostructures of g-C3N4 (such as nanosheets, nanodots, nanoribbons, nanospheres, and nanotubes) have been synthesized that offer more active sites for light-responsive reactions with enhanced electron-hole separation. Ansari et al. [41,42] reported the photo-booster effect of g-C3N4 NTs on the PV performance of cadmium sulfide QDs sensitized solar cells (QDSSC). The authors reported a 32% increase in PCE of g-C3N4 NTs-based devices compared to a g-C3N4 nanoflakes-based solar cell device. Tang group reported the CQDs-sensitized all-weather solar cells [8,9,43,44]. The maximized PCE achieved by this group was as high as 15.1% in a completely dark environment [44].

We employed the notion of co-atom doping to introduce sulfur (S) and oxygen (O) atoms to g-C3N4 nanotubes (O, S-g-C3N4 NTs) using a single precursor thiourea dioxide as an S and O source. The as-prepared O, S-g-C3N4 NTs were coupled with CQDs to obtain a high-performance light harvester for all-weather QDSSC. CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs composites were prepared by varying CQDs to O, S-g-C3N4 NTs weight ratio to optimize the CQDs loading in the photoanode. Light absorption and utilization are the common factors limiting the electricity generation of sensitized solar cells. Green long-persistence phosphors (LPPs) are optical energy-storing materials, which can store UV–Vis or IR light and excite visible light for a long time. Hence, incorporating LPP into TiO2 photoanode as a light absorber and placeholder would influence these all-weather QDSSC to generate electricity without compromising on the high efficiency during sunny days.
All the materials used in this study were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of O, S-doped g-C3N4 NTs
ce:section.ce:para: Bulk O, S-g-C3N4 NTs were prepared following the method provided in Ref. [45]. Briefly, 4 g of thiourea dioxide were placed in an alumina pot and heated in a muffle furnace at 450 °C at 10 °C/min for 12 h. The obtained powdered bulk O, S-g-C3N4 was exfoliated in ethanol by sonication for 8 h. The exfoliated O, S-g-C3N4 sheets were filtered and dispersed in water followed by heating at 350 °C for 30 min. The obtained O, S-g-C3N4 sheets were quenched in cold water and freeze-drying. Finally, O, S-g-C3N4 NTs were obtained by filtration and drying at 100 °C under vacuum. The synthesis of CQDs, coupling of CQDs and O, S-g-C3N4 NTs, preparation of photoanode and solar cell assembly, and characterization of prepared materials are discussed in the supporting information file (S–I section).

The photocurrent-voltage measurements of the fabricated devices were obtained using a potentiostat electrochemical workstation having a solar simulator (Peccell) with an AM 1.5 G filter under 100 mW/cm2 light intensity. We used a standard silicon PV solar cell to calibrate the light intensity. The active cell area was 0.25 cm2. Moreover, measurements of J-V curves were repeated at least ten times for each device to minimize the experimental errors. The solar cell devices were illuminated with the simulated sunlight for 1 min before measuring the dark J-V curves. The devices were then immediately kept in a dark environment, and the dark J-V curves were measured. The fluorescence intensities of FTO/TiO2/LPP films were recorded by standard silicon solar cells and used as light intensity for dark PV measurements.
(1)
P C E =
P max
/
P
i n
Equation (1) was used to measure the dark PV parameters, where Pin denotes the fluorescent intensity of FTO/TiO2/LPP film; its value was 57.6 μW/cm2.

O, S-g-C3N4 NTs were synthesized with a single precursor—thiourea oxide. They were prepared by a water-induced morphological transformation process (Fig. 1 ). Ethanol disperses O, S-g-C3N4 nanosheets and prevents the aggregations, resulting in improved morphology of NTs based on the roll-up mechanism of nanosheets. A class of 0-dimensional/2-dimensional (0D/2D) based on CQDs, and O, S-g-C3N4 NTs were designed as sensitizers for TiO2-based nanostructured solar cells. FTIR was carried out to determine the surface groups of CQDs. Fig. S1 shows the FTIR and Raman spectra of prepared CQDs. FTIR spectrum reveals that the absorption bands present at 3100–3500 cm−2, 2800–2900 cm−2, and 1600–1700 cm−2 are related to O–H stretching vibrations, C–H bending vibrations, and C=O stretching vibrations, respectively [46]. Raman spectroscopy was performed to analyze the defects in CQDs. The Raman spectrum shows the presence of two characteristic peaks at 1340 cm−2 and 1570 cm−2 which are attributed to the G and G band, respectively. The intensity ratio of D and G bands (ID/IG) of the prepared CQDs was 1.28 which shows that a large amount of amorphous carbon is present within the structure of synthesized CQDs [47]. The optical responses of all the fabricated samples were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrometer. Fig. 2 shows that the absorption edge for O, S-g-C3N4 NTs occurs at ∼462 nm. However, an obvious red shift in the absorption edge occurred compared to bare O, S-g-C3N4 NTs after coupling CQDs with O, S-g-C3N4 NTs. This can be attributed to the excellent light-harvesting property of CQDs and the porous structure of O, S-g-C3N4 NTs, which can promote the multiple reflections of incident light. The spectra of CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs samples (5-C/CN, 7-C/CN, and 9-C/CN) showed wide visible light absorption regions, demonstrating that CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs samples may be excited to generate more electron-hole pairs under the same irradiation conditions. Fig. 2b shows the images of 5-C/CN, 7-C/CN, and 9-C/CN samples with and without UV-light irradiations. Furthermore, the images show that the reagent solution changes from pale yellow (5-C/CN) to brown (9-C/CN). The emission color turned bright under UV irradiation increasing the CQDs concentrations. The energy band gap (Eg) was determined using the following formula:
(2)
α h v = A
( h v −
E g
)
n
,
where A, ν, α, Eg, and h denote a constant, frequency, absorption coefficient, energy band gap, and Plan's constant, respectively [48]. Fig. 2 c shows that the Eg of O, S-g-C3N4 NTs decreased from 2.68 to 2.43 after being decorated with CQDs. This indicates the presence of sub-band energy levels between CQDs and O, S-g-C3N4 NTs in the bandgaps of CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs composites [35]. The narrower Eg of sample 7-C/CN suggests the presence of more light-generated electrons under visible light irradiation than all other samples. PL spectra further proved the aforementioned assumptions (Fig. 2 d). Furthermore, Fig. 2 d shows that O, S-g-C3N4 NTs show a strong emission peak at approximately 460 nm, corresponding to the rapid irradiative recombination of electron-hole pairs. The intensity of emission peak decreased with increasing the CQDs contents, suggesting that the charge separation in O, S-g-C3N4 NTs was accelerated after being decorated with CQDs. The sample 7-C/CN showed the lowest PL intensity, indicating excellent charge separation; it can be attributed to the hollow porous tubular structure of O, S-g-C3N4 NTs and the presence of CQDs.

The XPS survey spectra and full resolution spectra of O, S-g-C3N4 NTs and 7-C/CN are shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. S2, respectively. Both O, S-g-C3N4 NTs (Figs. 3) and 7-C/CN (Fig. S2) were composed of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur, as shown in the spectra. A stronger oxygen peak in the 7-C/CN sample indicates higher oxygen content on the surface from water and O bonds. The O and S doping was confirmed by analyzing the high-resolution O1s and S2p spectra. The O1s spectra can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.6 and 532.7 eV attributed to C–O/N–C–O and C=O, respectively, suggesting that the oxygen atoms are adsorbed on the surface of g-C3N4 and show bonds with the sp [2] carbon by substituting N. The S2p spectra were convoluted into two peaks at 167.6 and 168.5 eV that corresponds to C–S and C-SOX species, respectively. C1s high-resolution spectra showed two characteristic peaks at 284.4 and 287.6, corresponding to C–C and C-(N)3, respectively. Furthermore, the high-resolution N1s spectra were deconvoluted into three peaks at 397.5398.2, and 399.7 attributed to pyridinic-N, C–N–C, and N-(C)3, respectively.

The morphology and nanostructures of as-synthesized materials were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM). Fig. 4 shows the TEM and HR-TEM images of O, S-g-C3N4 NTs (7-C/CN) decorated with as-prepared CQDs, O, S-g-C3N4 NTs, and CQDs. The as-prepared O, S-g-C3N4 NTs were hollow tubular nanostructures with open ends, demonstrating the successful fabrication of NTs via water-induced morphological transformation procedure (see Fig. 2a). The outer diameters of two individual O, S-g-C3N4 NTs were approximately 200 and 250 nm because of the nanosheets roll-up mechanism depending on the size and thickness of bent g-C3N4 nanosheets units (see the inset of Fig. 4a). The TEM image of CQDs in Fig. 4b shows that CQDs have spherical morphologies with some potential agglomerations. The inset image demonstrates an in-plane lattice spacing of 0.226 nm, similar to a previous report [49]. CQDs showed uniform size distribution with varying diameters, ranging from 1.1 to 4.2 nm (average diameter = 2.3 nm). Fig. 4c shows the TEM image of CQDs-decorated O, S-g-C3N4 NTs (7-C/CN) sample. The image and the inset show that the spherical CQDs are well dispersed on the surface of O, S-g-C3N4 NTs.

XRD was performed to analyze the crystal structures and phases of the prepared samples. Fig. 5 shows the XRD patterns of CQDs, O, S-g-C3N4 NTs, and CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs samples. CQDs exhibited a broad peak at 2θ = 25.1°, demonstrating the amorphous framework of CQDs. These observations suggest that the obtained CQDs consist of a graphitic core and amorphous carbon on the surface. However, O, S-g-C3N4 NTs and CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs samples displayed two diffraction peaks centered at 2θ = 13.1° and 2θ = 27.6°. These peaks can be indexed as the graphitic carbon nitride for (100) and (102) crystal facets, representing an inter-planner accumulation of aromatic rings and in-plane repeated tri-s-triazine units, respectively [19,39]. The CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs samples also displayed a weaker peak at 2θ = 25.1°, corresponding to CQDs. The peak intensity slightly increased with the increasing loading amount of CQDs.

The high surface area with porosity of any photoanodic material is the key feature to be used in PV devices for better PV performance. Fig. S3 and Table S1 show the N2 adsorption-desorption and the pore size distribution (inset) for 5-C/CN, 7-C/CN, and 9-C/CN composite samples. The appearance of a hysteresis loop in the isotherm reveals the mesoporous nature of composite samples. All the samples show type-IV isotherms. The observed BET surface area values for 5-C/CN, 7-C/CN, and 9-C/CN are estimated to be 118.82, 126.56, and 114.98 m2/g, respectively. The higher BET surface area values for 7-C/CN suggest that it offers more active sites for sensitizer loading leading to better performance of the device [50].

Fig. 6 a represents the all-weather solar cell assembly. CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs can move into the layer and combine with TiO2 owing to the large specific surface area of the mesoporous TiO2 layer. The excited electron could move to the conduction band of TiO2 upon irradiation and transfer to the external circuit [44]. Fig. 7 a shows the transmittance spectra of FTO/TiO2 and all the prepared photoanodes. The transmittance spectrum of FTO/TiO2 began at approximately 368 nm, and it was 54.8% at λ = 600 nm. After loading CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs to the TiO2 layer, the transmittance was ∼0 for λ < 400 nm and gradually increased with the light wavelength. After introducing the LPP to the photoanode as a light placeholder, the remaining optical transmittance was 13.6%. Ultimately, 41.2% of unabsorbed light was further converted to visible light at 600 nm wavelength. We demonstrated that LPP stored the unabsorbed light for green emission. Furthermore, Fig. 6b shows the PL images of CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs/LPP-sensitized photoanodes taken after irradiation with simulated sunlight. The external bright rectangles denote the green-emitting LPPs, while the dark inner rectangles refer to CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs/LPP-sensitized photoanodes. We conclude from the images that LLP can reutilize the unabsorbed light and emit the green fluorescent light, partially absorbed by the CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs for photogenerated electrons. As discussed above, LPP can absorb and convert the permeated light into green fluorescence across the TiO2 layer for λ > 400; therefore, the PL emission spectra of CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs/LPP-sensitized photoanodes were taken at the excitation wavelength (λex) of 450 nm to analyze the sensitivity of CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs under dark conditions. Fig. 7b shows that the spectra of all the prepared photoanodes exhibit broad emission peaks around 500–515 nm when excited with 450 nm wavelength, which is attributed to the PL emission of LPP. Furthermore, the released fluorescence was reabsorbed by CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs. In this regard, LPP can expand the absorption spectrum to infrared light.

Fig. 8 shows the photo and dark J-V curves of the fabricated solar cell devices, which comprised of CQDs, O, S-g-C3N4 NTs, and CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs-sensitized photoanodes. Pt counter electrode (CE),
I −
/
I 3 −
redox electrolyte, and the measured PV parameters are summarized in Table 1
. The CQDs and O, S-g-C3N4 NTs-sensitized devices showed PCE of 0.28% and 0.24% under 100 mW light intensity illumination (see Table 1). However, the solar cell devices that were sensitized with CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs showed significant improvement in PV performance, yielding the PCE of 0.83%, 0.95%, and 0.91% for devices 5-CC, 7-CC, and 9-CC, respectively. O, S-g-C3N4 NTs acted as photo-booster as well as a barrier to prevent the backflow of photoexcited electrons at electrode/electrolyte interface. Higher Voc values of the devices sensitized with CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs could be attributed to the high surface area of NTs which generate more active sites to deposit CQDs resulted in better charge transfer and lower recombination rate of photoexcited electrons. After introducing LPP to the photoanodes, the PV performance of the devices was improved further. Moreover, 5-CC/L, 7-CC/L, and 9-CC/L showed PCE of 1.30%, 1.13%, and 1.06%, respectively, under 100 mw light intensity illumination. The statistical distributions of PCE without and with LPP under simulated light irradiation are given in Fig. S4 and Fig. S5, respectively. However, the measured PCEs of these devices were much lower than state-of-the-art solar cell devices due to the weak affinity of CQDs and O, S-g-C3N4 NTs with the surface of TiO2 layers. The devices 5-CC, 7-CC, and 9-CC showed zero power outputs when the simulated sunlight was removed. On the contrary, 5-CC/L, 7-CC/L, and 9-CC/L devices showed remarkable performance under dark conditions. As shown in Fig. 8c, the J-V curves do not pass through the origin coordinates (see Table 2).
(3)
P C E
( % )
= P
/
max
P
i n
× 100 % =
(
J
s c
×
V
o c
× F F )
/
P
i n
× 100 % ,
where Pmax denotes maximal output power, and Pin represents the fluorescence intensity of the TiO2/CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs/LPP photoanode. The fluorescence intensity was measured to be 57.6 μW/cm2 (Fig. 10 a). Under dark atmosphere, the final PCEs of the 5-CC/L, 7-CC/L, and 9-CC/L were reached 16.4%, 18.5%, and 15.9%, respectively. Fig. S5 shows the statistical distribution of PCE with LPP under a dark atmosphere. Hence, the power generation under dark conditions also obeys the photoelectric theory under sunlight. The power generation mechanism of the solar cells containing CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs/LPP-sensitized photoanodes under light and dark conditions is provided in Fig. S7 and Fig. S8, respectively. Fig. S7 demonstrates that the conduction band (CB) positions of O, S-g-C3N4 NTs (−3.38) and CQDs (−3.47) are more negative compared to TiO2 (−4.23), which demonstrates that the backflow of photoinduced electrons from TiO2 to CQDs/O and S-g-C3N4 NTs is energetically impossible. Fig. S8 shows that CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs can synergistically collect photons from green emission upon irradiating by the fluorescence light of CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs/LPP photoanode and then transfer electrons from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) to the CB of TiO2, which are transported to the FTO layer transferring holes to
I −
/
I 3 −
redox couples. We conclude that the fabricated all-weather solar cells can store solar energy from unabsorbed incident light and subsequent green emission.

The long-term stability of all-weather solar cells is equally important to PCE. The persistent green fluorescence from the LPPs determines the photo-to-electric conversion process. The photoluminescence images of prepared CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs/LPP photoanodes were captured after illuminating them with simulated sunlight for 1 min. The images were taken immediately or after 5, 20, 30, 60, and 120 min (Fig. 6b). Fig. 10a shows the afterglow intensity of TiO2/LPP photoanode as a function of decay time. The green fluorescence intensity reduced from 57.6 to 7.07 μW/cm2 within 2 min, as seen in Fig. 10a. The persistent emission from TiO2/LPP photoanode lasted up to 5 h at low. The persistent luminescence is crucial for power generation in all-weather solar cells. The dark J-V cures of 5-CC/L, 7-CC/L, and 9-CC/L solar cell devices recorded at various decay times are shown in Fig. 9 , and the corresponding dark PV parameters as a function of decay time are shown in fig. 10 b–e and Table S2–Table S4. The initial short-circuit current (Jsc), open-circuit voltage (Voc), and fill factor (FF) of all-weather solar cells reduced after a decay period of 2 h: from 36.40 μA/cm2, 0.39 V, and 67.51 to 13.00 μA/cm2, 0.241 V, and 42.25 for 5-CC/L; from 34.81 μA/cm2, 0.39 V, and 76.91 to 16.08 μA, 0.25 V, and 46.71% for 7-CC/L; and from 37.21 μA, 037 V, 66.21% to 10.81 μA, 0.23 V, and 41.35% for 9-CC/L, respectively. A comparison of the obtained efficiency under 100 mw/cm2 light intensity and under dark condition is given in Table 3 . The superior performance of the 7-CC/L device may be due to the stronger synergistic effects between the CQDs and O, S-g-C3N4 NTs composite, the high surface area of O, S-g-C3N4 NTs which generate more active sites to load CQDs resulted in enhanced light harvesting ability, and the longer diffusion length of photoinduced electrons. The prepared all-weather solar cells showed excellent performance at short decay times; however, the solar cell devices showed relatively low electricity generation at longer decay times. Therefore, it is important to develop persistent visible light-emitting LPPs with high fluorescence intensities.

We demonstrated a facile approach for excellent charge separation and injection in CQDs-sensitized all-weather solar cells by combining a low-cost organic semiconductor O, S-g-C3N4 NTs with light storing/emission material and long-persistent phosphor. The prepared all-weather solar cell delivered a maximum PCE of 18.5% under a dark environment. Moreover, the devices could generate electricity for several hours. It was observed that the O, S-g-C3N4 NTs acted as a barrier for reverse tunneling of photoinduced electrons at the working electrode/electrolyte interface, resulting in the boosted PV performance of the device. Further, the better performance of solar cell devices can also be attributed to the high surface area of O, S-g-C3N4 NTs which generate more active sites to load CQDs resulted in enhanced light harvesting ability. In summary, the PV performance of the prepared all-weather solar cells was obtained due to the following factors: minimum photoinduced electrons interception, optimum loading of CQDs/O, S-g-C3N4 NTs sensitizer and LPP, and the stronger interface between the sensitizer and TiO2. The observed results and concepts of all-weather solar cells led us to the fundamental question on ways to further increase the dark PCE and stability and explore the all-weather advanced PVs for electricity generation.
Shahina Riaz: conceptualization, methodology, software, data curation, writing - original draft preparation. Soo-Jin Park: visualization, investigation, supervision, writing - reviewing and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
